reviewer: new-demo build wave (in-browser runnability proof first, then headless-Chrome real download + real inference on the index page via CDP driver, mobile+desktop overflow + console/network checks, before/after visual review)

rubric.evidence: REAL in-browser inference proven BEFORE building: an ORT-web@1.21.0 WASM harness (headless Chrome, no GPU) loaded deblurring_nafnet_2025may.onnx — session input 'lq' [1,3,512,512] f32, output 'output' [1,3,512,512] f32 (256×256 is rejected → the network is fixed at 512). On a motion-blurred street scene the restored output is visibly and dramatically sharper (lamp, arches, window frames, cobblestones — screenshot reviewed), corr(in,out)=0.98 (same scene) and mean|out−in|=0.025 (real change, not identity). transformers.js has no `nafnet` class and no deblurring pipeline task, so the demo runs the ONNX graph directly via raw ONNX Runtime Web in a module Worker. Headless page build-verify (CDP driver): fresh profile → Download (~92 MB) → ready → Restore produced real output, inference 15382 ms + composite 9 ms on WASM, Laplacian-variance sharpness 0.0020 → 0.0023 (rose), 0 console errors, 0 network failures.
rubric.notes: NAFNet (ECCV 2022, OpenCV Model Zoo, MIT) is a restoration network trained on GoPro motion blur — it reconstructs detail, not a contrast boost.

rubric.evidence: Output shape + semantic sanity confirmed: output tensor dims [1,3,512,512] in [0,1] read back at runtime; clamped to RGBA and resized to the display aspect. Semantic check: the restored image is sharper than the blurred input (visual wipe + a rise in Laplacian-variance sharpness), and the 'See inside' restoration-delta map lights up along edges/text (where a blur destroys detail) and stays dark on flat areas — proving the change tracks structure, not noise.
rubric.notes: image-to-image task template (dense per-pixel output grounded in the input) fits: restored RGB from the blurred RGB.

rubric.evidence: At-a-glance names the exact model (opencv/deblurring_nafnet, linked), task (image-to-image / deblurring), runtime (onnxruntime-web 1.21), input 'lq' [1,3,512,512] / output [1,3,512,512] shapes, dtype (fp32, plain WASM EP, no WebGPU), size (~92 MB) and licence (MIT), and honestly notes transformers.js has no NAFNet class. inference-off-main-thread = pass (new Worker + worker.js). No invented API.
rubric.notes: Fixed 512×512 network input is stated up front; arbitrary images are stretched to 512 and the output resized back.

rubric.evidence: Every page loads through createModelLoader. The worker fetches the ONNX THROUGH Cache Storage (nafnet-deblur-onnx-cache) with a content-length Response so lib/model-cache.js scans '/opencv/deblurring_nafnet/' and the shared loader auto-inits on a returning visit (no Download button); the per-model clear-cache control works. First-visit driver run showed the honest Download (~92 MB) state, streaming progress, then ready.
rubric.notes: The 4 ladder pages share the same modelId → shared cache → auto-init; the multi-model pages load a SECOND model each through its own loader.

rubric.evidence: loads-clean = pass (0 console errors on load). Download progress streams from the worker into the loader progress bar; failures surface in the .status.err line and the loader offers Retry. Status uses role=status aria-live=polite. runs-without-webgpu-fallback: WASM-only (requiresWebGPU:false) — runs everywhere, verified on the no-GPU headless runner. A 512×512 pass is ~15 s on single-threaded WASM; the UI says so and disables the button with a working status.
rubric.notes: The blur controls only rebuild the preview (cheap); inference runs only on Restore.

rubric.evidence: Every control exercised and labelled + keyboard-operable: the rights-safe example gallery, the drop-zone + file input, the Restore / Download buttons, the blur-type <select> and blur-amount <input type=range>, and the before/after reveal (a native <input type=range> with aria-valuetext '% restored'). Blur controls live-update the preview; the demo blurs a bounded working copy so `amount` px is meaningful at the model's 512 input (uploads use the real blur as-is).
rubric.notes: Honest by design — the user chooses the demo blur and watches NAFNet undo it; real blurry uploads are supported.

rubric.evidence: The restored image, the restoration-delta map, and the sharpness numbers all come from the on-device ORT-web run — no canned values. The output changes with the input blur and content (visible in the wipe and the delta map), proving it tracks the real model, not a placeholder.
rubric.notes: The copy states plainly that NAFNet can't recover information a blur erased entirely — a rescue, not a time machine — and the wild page shows the recovery falling as blur grows.

rubric.evidence: images-have-alt = pass, buttons-have-names = pass, live-status-region = pass. Canvases carry role=img + descriptive aria-label (blurred input / restored output / restoration delta); the reveal slider is a labelled native range with aria-valuetext; the blur-type select and amount slider have <label>s; gallery thumbnails carry source/licence in their accessible name. Status is role=status aria-live=polite. :focus-visible styled on the reveal, drop-zone, and range.
rubric.notes: Guidance applied for the accessible comparison slider and canvas alternatives (see guidanceConsulted).

rubric.evidence: responsive-desktop = pass, responsive-mobile = pass. CDP driver measured horizontal overflow = 0 px at BOTH desktop 1280×800 and mobile 360×740 DPR3 on the index page. Layout uses flex:1 1 300px + min-inline-size:0 two-column (image | controls) that stacks on mobile; the gallery is an auto-fill minmax grid; canvases are inline-size:100% block-size:auto; the reveal uses logical properties + touch-action:pan-y. Light + dark inherit design-system variables.
rubric.notes: The multi-model panels use the same flex two-column so the before/after detection canvases stack on mobile.

rubric.evidence: Inference AND the dense output composite (clamp tensor → RGBA, restoration-delta map, resize to display aspect — the systemic hotspot invariant 15 warns about) run in the module Worker; the restored image and delta map are built with OffscreenCanvas and returned as transferred ImageBitmaps (zero-copy). The main thread only draws bitmaps. Measured composite 9 ms in the worker, so no main-thread long task during interaction.
rubric.notes: Guidance applied: break-up-long-tasks / identify-inp-causes — the per-pixel work is off the main thread and transferred, not cloned.

guidanceConsulted.query: accessible range slider input keyboard labels aria-valuetext, canvas role img alt, live status region, select label
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Prefer native controls with <label>; a range input is keyboard-operable and should announce its value (aria-valuetext); give informative canvases role=img + a descriptive name; use a single polite live region; style :focus-visible.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — the before/after divider is a native <input type=range> with aria-valuetext ('N% restored'); the blur-type <select> and amount range have <label>s; every canvas has role=img + a descriptive aria-label; a single role=status aria-live=polite reports progress/results; :focus-visible rings throughout.

guidanceConsulted.query: responsive control panel two column image + controls no horizontal overflow flexbox intrinsic sizing logical properties
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Use flexbox with flex:1 1 <basis> + min-inline-size:0 so wide flex items shrink instead of overflowing; size media with inline-size:100% block-size:auto; use logical properties; auto-fill/minmax grids for galleries.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — image/controls columns use flex:1 1 300px with min-inline-size:0 and stack on narrow viewports; the example gallery is a repeat(auto-fill,minmax()) grid; canvases and the reveal are inline-size:100% block-size:auto. Verified 0 px horizontal overflow at 360×740 and 1280×800.

guidanceConsulted.query: keep INP low move image inference and dense per-pixel post-processing off main thread web worker transfer
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Move long main-thread JavaScript (inference + dense pixel loops) into a worker; transfer buffers/ImageBitmaps instead of cloning.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — ONNX inference, the RGBA clamp, the restoration-delta map, and the display-aspect resize (the dense-output hotspot) all run in a module worker; results are transferred back as ImageBitmaps. No blocking main-thread pixel loop during interaction.

openQuestions: NAFNet is fixed at 512×512; a follow-up could tile large images with overlap so high-resolution photos are restored at full detail rather than downscaled.
A WebGPU ORT-web EP path would cut the ~15 s WASM pass substantially — worth a progressive-enhancement follow-up once verifiable on a GPU runner.

followUpGoals.goal: Add a super-resolution follow-on (deblur → Swin2SR upscale) as a restoration pipeline ladder demo.
Add a live-camera 'steady-cam' wild demo that restores each captured frame (user-initiated, lib/capture-ux.js) to show hand-held shots cleaned up.

summary: New demo: on-device image DEBLURRING / restoration via NAFNet (opencv/deblurring_nafnet, MIT, OpenCV Model Zoo) run through raw ONNX Runtime Web in a module worker, with the dense output composite (clamp + restoration-delta + resize) off the main thread and transferred as ImageBitmaps. Runnability was proven first in headless Chrome (fixed 512×512 'lq'→'output', visibly dramatic deblur on a motion-blurred street scene); the index + 4 ladder pages (basics blur-then-restore / practical document rescue+download / wild breaking-point / multi-model deblur→text-detection) were build-verified with real download + inference, an accessible before/after reveal, cached auto-init, clean console, and 0 px overflow on mobile+desktop. Sample images come from the rights-safe media library; demo blur is applied at a bounded working scale so it is meaningful at the model input, and real blurry photos can be uploaded.
